title: Semantic content review

publication.changeSummary: Technology-specific HTML: Semantic and Accessible Web Documents draft for Semantic content review; independent standards, browser, pedagogy, and accessibility review required. Beginner-quality contract 1.0.0 applied deterministically from existing source-linked draft facts; publication stage and reviewer state are unchanged.

sources.locator: Web Content Accessibility Guidelines 2.2; W3C Recommendation 2023; provisions applicable to Semantic content review
sources.claim: Content review checks headings, link purpose, plain language, alternatives, instructions, errors, and reading order against real user tasks.

sources.locator: HTML Living Standard; living standard accessed 2026-07; provisions applicable to Semantic content review
sources.claim: Content review checks headings, link purpose, plain language, alternatives, instructions, errors, and reading order against real user tasks.

canonicalExplanation.summary: Content review checks headings, link purpose, plain language, alternatives, instructions, errors, and reading order against real user tasks.

sections.heading: Start here
sections.body: Start with this case: A reviewer replaces three Learn more links with destination-specific names and simplifies an unexplained acronym. Your goal is to produce a traceable review checklist and repair semantic inconsistencies. Work through the example one step at a time, then use the boundary to check your answer.

sections.heading: Before you begin
sections.body: Review these declared prerequisites first: Metadata and accessibility audit; Images and media; Data tables; Forms and native controls; Markup conformance; Keyboard and accessibility-tree audit. If one is unfamiliar, revisit it before continuing.

sections.heading: Technology rule
sections.body: Content review checks headings, link purpose, plain language, alternatives, instructions, errors, and reading order against real user tasks. Apply this rule to the actual markup, stylesheet, JavaScript, or component state rather than memorizing its name.

sections.heading: Boundary and browser reality
sections.body: Correct syntax cannot guarantee comprehensible wording, and accessibility depends on language and context as well as elements. This draft therefore rejects: Semantic elements automatically make the words inside them clear and accessible.

includes: Review page purpose, outline, landmarks, link names, media alternatives, tables and forms together.
Produce a traceable review checklist and repair semantic inconsistencies.
excludes: Correct syntax cannot guarantee comprehensible wording, and accessibility depends on language and context as well as elements.

outcomes: Produce a traceable review checklist and repair semantic inconsistencies.

examples.title: Semantic content review: concrete case
examples.situation: A reviewer replaces three Learn more links with destination-specific names and simplifies an unexplained acronym.
walkthrough: Step 1 — Inspect the exact artifact and starting state: A reviewer replaces three Learn more links with destination-specific names and simplifies an unexplained acronym.
Step 2 — Use the core rule above to predict one observable result.
Step 3 — Verify the correction: Review the actual language and task context; semantic markup supports but does not create understandable content.
examples.result: The result follows because Review the actual language and task context; semantic markup supports but does not create understandable content.

examples.title: Semantic content review: boundary case
examples.situation: Correct syntax cannot guarantee comprehensible wording, and accessibility depends on language and context as well as elements.
walkthrough: Step 1 — Start from the documented boundary: Correct syntax cannot guarantee comprehensible wording, and accessibility depends on language and context as well as elements.
Step 2 — Use the core rule above to predict one observable result.
Step 3 — Reject the misconception: Semantic elements automatically make the words inside them clear and accessible.
examples.result: The boundary requires this correction: Review the actual language and task context; semantic markup supports but does not create understandable content.

misconceptions.diagnosticPrompt: Use a concrete HTML: Semantic and Accessible Web Documents artifact to explain why this belief fails: Semantic elements automatically make the words inside them clear and accessible.

analogy.example: A labeled document outline can model Semantic content review, with structure carrying meaning before decoration.
usefulFor: Building a first mental model for Semantic content review before using exact platform terms
limitations: Documents cannot model parser recovery, browser APIs, or interactive semantics exactly.

recap.summary: You should now be able to produce a traceable review checklist and repair semantic inconsistencies. Keep this boundary in view: Correct syntax cannot guarantee comprehensible wording, and accessibility depends on language and context as well as elements.

Apply checkpoint 'actual language' to a new artifact.
recap.nextReviewPrompt: Next: continue to “Progressive baseline”. Before moving on, answer one retrieval prompt without reopening the explanation.
